title: ML Model Risks and Mitigations
concept:
Once a model is trained, it carries specific risks that must be addressed through targeted testing. Understanding the mapping from risk to mitigation is foundational to ML model testing.

| **Risk** | **Testing Mitigation** |
|---|---|
| Biased model | Bias testing (disparate impact analysis) |
| Adversarial examples | Adversarial testing (black-box/white-box) |
| Overfitting | Overfitting testing (train vs test accuracy gap) |
| Underfitting | Underfitting testing (learning curve analysis) |
| Data/concept drift | Drift testing (K-S test, output monitoring) |
| Unacceptable performance | ML functional performance testing (statistical) |
| Test oracle problem | Metamorphic testing + back-to-back testing + A/B testing |
| Security/safety/privacy | Red teaming |
| Poor documentation | Model documentation review |
| Deployment regression | Back-to-back testing + A/B testing + smoke testing |

Each risk requires a different testing technique. Some risks (test oracle problem) require combining multiple techniques. The choice of technique also depends on the available information: adversarial testing can be white-box (model internals known) or black-box (model internals unknown).

Risk prioritisation determines the order and depth of testing. High-impact risks in safety-critical systems (adversarial examples in an autonomous vehicle) receive the most testing effort.
keyPoints: Each ML model risk maps to a specific testing mitigation: adversarial examples → adversarial testing; drift → drift testing; oracle problem → metamorphic + back-to-back + A/B testing.
The test oracle problem requires combining multiple techniques (metamorphic, back-to-back, A/B) since no single technique fully resolves the absence of expected outputs.
Risk prioritisation drives testing depth — safety-critical model risks receive the most testing effort under a risk-based approach.

quiz.question: An ML model for autonomous vehicle lane detection is being tested. The team identifies 'adversarial examples' as a risk. Which testing approach directly addresses this risk?
options: Overfitting testing — comparing train accuracy to validation accuracy.
Model documentation review — verifying that adversarial examples are documented in the model card.
Adversarial testing — generating minimally perturbed inputs designed to cause misclassification and testing whether the model is vulnerable.
Drift testing — monitoring whether the input distribution changes after deployment.
quiz.explanation: Adversarial examples are inputs with imperceptible perturbations that cause misclassification. Adversarial testing directly addresses this risk by generating such inputs and verifying whether the model maintains correct behaviour. Documentation review is important but does not test the model itself; drift testing addresses distribution shift, not adversarial manipulation.
